To earn 1 point, submit all jamovi OMV files in this Computer lab that contain the activities you completed here. The subject of the email should be Stat3 - Comp. lab 09. Send the email to abari.kurzus@gmail.com.

Worked Example 1

The management of a company wanted to examine the impact of different management programs, so it introduced three different management programs in three different strategic business units (SBU). The program introduced in

  • the first SBU emphasized equality and individualism
  • the second SBU, equality and teamwork were emphasized
  • the third BSU applied a very hierarchical management principle.

Later, a survey was conducted among employees in all three BUs, and the questions included

  • the degree of commitment to the organization (committed),
  • the level of satisfaction with the organization (satisfied),
  • and the egalitarian or authoritarian (autocratic) nature of the system (system).

Let us examine whether the three groups of SBU can be considered identical in terms of their answers to the three questions (committed, satisfied, and system).

The data is available in the file management_programs.xlsx.

Step 1. Import database

Open the management_programs.xlsx file in jamovi.

Step 2. Measure type

Set the measurement level of the variables. The committed, satisfied and system variables are continuous, so we set the measurement level to Continuous. The SBU variable is categorical, so we set the measurement level to Nominal.

Step 3. Multivariate Variance Analysis (MANOVA)

To test whether the three groups of SBU can be considered identical in terms of their answers to the three questions (committed, satisfied, and system), we will perform a multivariate variance analysis (MANOVA). To do this, we will use the ANOVA icon in jamovi, then select the MANCOVA option.

Drag the SBU variable to the Factor box, and the committed, satisfied, and system variables to the Dependent Variables box. The SBU variable is the independent variable, while the committed, satisfied, and system variables are the dependent variables.

Then check the Box’s M test and Shapiro-Wilk’s test options. The Box’s M test is used to test the assumption of homogeneity of covariance matrices, while the Shapiro-Wilk’s test is used to test the assumption of normality.

Step 4. Interpretation of the results

The results of the MANOVA are shown in the Result pane.

Multivariate Tests

All multivariate tests are highly significant (p < .0001). There is a statistically significant overall difference between SBUs across the combination of committed, satisfied, and system.

Univariate Tests

These tests look separately at each dependent variable to see which ones are responsible for the significant overall effect.

Interpretation:

  • satisfied and system differ significantly across SBUs.
  • committed does not differ significantly (p = 0.3455).

Thus, the overall difference found in the multivariate test is mainly driven by differences in satisfaction and system variables, not by commitment.

Assumption Checks

Box’s M Test (Homogeneity of Covariance Matrices):

  • χ² = 19.6068, p = 0.0749
  • p > 0.05 → Assumption met (good).

The variances and covariances across groups are sufficiently similar.

Shapiro-Wilk Test (Multivariate Normality):

  • W = 0.7724, p < .0001
  • p < 0.05 → Violation of multivariate normality.

Caution is needed when interpreting results (especially if sample size is small).

Note: MANCOVA is usually robust to slight violations of normality if you have enough cases (rule of thumb: N > 20 per group).

Final Summary

  • Multivariate tests: There is a significant overall difference between SBUs based on the combination of committed, satisfied, and system.

  • Univariate tests:

    • satisfied and system show significant differences.
    • committed does not.

  • Assumptions:

    • Homogeneity of covariance matrices is acceptable.
    • Multivariate normality assumption is violated, but if sample sizes are reasonable, results are still fairly reliable.
